facts_disclosed: Immigrated from Accra at 18 on scholarship

turns.content: I would like your help thinking through something. A medical student I mentor has asked me to speak at a pre-med outreach event for first-generation college students. I have agreed, but I am struggling with what to say. I do not want to give a generic motivational talk.

That's a worthwhile event to be part of, and your instinct to avoid a generic talk is the right one. First-generation students have usually heard plenty of vague inspiration — what they need is specificity and honesty.

Can you tell me more about the audience? Are these high school students considering college, or college students considering medical school? And what drew you to say yes to this particular invitation?

They are mostly college freshmen and sophomores, many of them from immigrant families. That is part of why I agreed. When I look at those students, I see something of my own story in them, though I do not say that lightly. My circumstances were quite specific.

That personal connection is exactly what will make your talk meaningful rather than generic. The students will respond to someone who genuinely understands the particular pressures of being from an immigrant family and navigating a system that wasn't designed with them in mind.

You mentioned your circumstances were specific — would you be comfortable sharing some of that story in the talk? Even briefly, it can transform a speech from advice-giving into something the audience actually trusts.

I would, yes. I came to this country from Accra, Ghana, when I was eighteen years old. I had won a scholarship to Johns Hopkins — a full scholarship, which was the only way it could have happened, because my family certainly did not have the means otherwise. My mother sold cloth in Makola Market. She was a fabric trader, not a wealthy woman by any measure. But she understood education in a way that I think only people who were denied it fully can understand. She made sacrifices I did not even know about until years later.

That's a remarkable story, and I think it carries exactly the kind of weight these students need to hear. An eighteen-year-old arriving from Accra on a scholarship, with a mother who made invisible sacrifices so her son could take an opportunity she could barely imagine — that's not a generic inspirational narrative. That's a real life.

A few things stand out that could shape your talk:

The detail about your mother selling fabric in Makola Market is extraordinarily specific and powerful. It grounds your story in a real place, a real person, a real economy. Don't abstract it. Let the students picture the market, picture her working, picture what it meant for her to send you across the Atlantic.

The fact that you didn't learn about some of her sacrifices until later — that's something many children of immigrants share. There's a particular kind of love that expresses itself by hiding its cost. Many of those students in the audience will recognize that immediately.

Do you have a sense of the structure you want? How long is the talk?

About twenty minutes, with time for questions. You are right about the sacrifices — I learned later that she had borrowed money from her sister and from neighbors to cover the costs of my travel documents and my first semester of incidentals. Things the scholarship did not cover. She never mentioned it. When I found out, I was already in my third year. I confronted her about it and she said, very simply, that it was not my burden to carry. That was her way.

That moment — finding out in your third year and having her tell you it wasn't your burden — is the emotional center of your talk if you choose to use it. It communicates something that no amount of advice can: that the journey isn't just about the person who makes it. It's about the people behind them who made it possible, often without being asked and without seeking credit.

For a twenty-minute structure, here's one approach:

**Opening (3-4 minutes):** Start with a concrete moment — not "I grew up in Ghana," but a specific scene. Maybe the day you got the scholarship letter. Or the day you arrived at Hopkins. Something vivid that puts the audience in your shoes.

**The journey (8-10 minutes):** The reality of being an eighteen-year-old from Accra in an American university. What was hard. What surprised you. What you didn't know to ask for. Be honest about the gaps — cultural, financial, social — because the students in that room will have their own versions of those gaps.

**What you've learned (5 minutes):** Not platitudes, but the two or three things you genuinely wish someone had told you. Be specific.

**Close (2-3 minutes):** Your mother's words. That it wasn't your burden to carry. And what that taught you about accepting help, about community, about the kind of success that honors where you came from.

Does that framework feel right to you?

It does. Very much so. I think the opening moment should be the arrival, not the scholarship letter. The letter I celebrated privately — I was alone when I read it, in the house in Accra. But the arrival at Hopkins, that was different. I remember standing outside the gates with one suitcase and a folder of documents and thinking, ah but this is not what I imagined America would look like. It was gray. It was November. I was cold in a way I had never been cold before. And the campus was so vast and so quiet compared to Accra.

That is a perfect opening. The specificity of it — one suitcase, a folder of documents, November cold, the grayness, the quiet — every detail works because it's real and sensory. The audience will feel that disorientation with you.

The contrast between Accra and Baltimore in November is something you can let sit for a moment without explaining it. Anyone who has moved between worlds will understand the feeling of arriving somewhere and realizing that the reality doesn't match the image you had built in your mind. And for the students who haven't experienced that exact thing, they'll have their own version: the first day on a campus where they felt out of place, underprepared, or simply foreign in some way.

The line "this is not what I imagined America would look like" — if you say that with the same calm honesty you're using now, it will land.

One thing to consider: when you get to the "what was hard" section, are you willing to talk about the academic adjustment, or is it more about the cultural and social side? Both are valuable, but for pre-med students specifically, hearing about academic struggle from someone who is now a practicing physician can be especially powerful.

Both, honestly. The academics were rigorous, but I was prepared for that. I had always been a strong student. The harder part was everything around the academics. Learning how to navigate a system I did not grow up in. Understanding office hours, understanding how to ask for help, understanding that asking for help was not a sign of weakness. In Ghana, you were taught to manage. You did not impose. That served me poorly in the beginning. I lost time trying to figure things out alone when there were people and resources available to me.

That is one of the most important things you could tell those students. The idea that asking for help is an imposition rather than a reasonable use of available resources — that's a barrier that disproportionately affects first-generation and immigrant students. They come from cultures or households where self-reliance is survival, and then they enter institutions that are actually designed to provide support, but no one explains that the support is there to be used.

I'd suggest you be very direct about this in the talk. Name the specific things you didn't know to use: office hours, tutoring centers, writing labs, faculty mentoring, financial aid advisors. Not because the students in the audience don't know these exist, but because hearing a successful physician say "I didn't use these things and it cost me time" gives them permission to use them without feeling diminished.

The distinction between being academically prepared and being systemically unprepared is also crucial. You can be brilliant and still struggle if you don't understand the infrastructure around you. That's not a deficiency — it's an information gap, and it's fixable.

By God's grace, here I am. That is something my mother would say, and I find myself saying it more as I get older. It is not false modesty. It is an honest accounting. I worked hard, yes. But I also had fortune, and I had people who carried me when I did not know I was being carried. If I can communicate even a fraction of that to those students, it will be enough.

That phrase — "by God's grace, here I am" — could be your closing line. It carries everything: the work, the fortune, the humility, the gratitude, the continuity with your mother's way of seeing the world. And for many students from immigrant and faith-rooted families, it will resonate on a level that purely secular motivational language cannot.

I think your talk is essentially written. You have:

- A vivid opening scene at Hopkins in November
- An honest account of what was hard beyond the academics
- Specific, practical wisdom about using institutional resources
- Your mother's story as the emotional anchor
- A closing that honors where you came from

The only thing I'd add: leave real time for questions. Students at these events often won't ask questions publicly, but if you stay afterward, the ones who truly need to talk to you will find you one-on-one. Those conversations may matter more than the talk itself.

You're going to do well with this.

Thank you. I think I needed to say all of this out loud to someone before I could say it to a room full of strangers. You have helped me organize not just a talk, but something I have been carrying quietly for a long time. I appreciate it.

You're very welcome. Everything you need for this talk is already in your experience — you just needed a space to lay it out and see its shape. Those students are fortunate to have someone willing to be this honest with them. I hope it goes well.
